title: AMI + Auto Scaling Group + Load Balancing

sections.title: 🖼️ AMI — Amazon Machine Image
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Fleet Big Picture Diagram
```
AMI
 │
 ▼
Launch Template
 │
 ▼
Auto Scaling Group
 │
 ├────────► EC2 A
 ├────────► EC2 B
 └────────► EC2 C
              │
              ▼
             ALB
              │
              ▼
            Users

MENTAL MODEL:
AMI defines one server image.
ASG creates and heals the fleet.
ALB sends traffic only to healthy servers.
```

### What is an AMI?
```
AMI = the template used to launch EC2 instances
Contains:
→ Operating system (Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, Windows)
→ Pre-installed software (nginx, Node.js, Java, your app)
→ Configuration files
→ EBS volume mappings (which volumes to attach, what size)

ANALOGY:
AMI = a master copy (like a ghost image) of a computer
Launch 100 EC2 instances from same AMI → all 100 are identical clones
Perfect for: Auto Scaling groups, consistent deployments

WITHOUT CUSTOM AMI:
Launch EC2 → run User Data script → install everything → takes 2–5 minutes
→ Auto Scaling event → new instance → waits 2–5 min before serving traffic

WITH CUSTOM AMI (golden image):
Pre-bake everything into AMI → launch → ready in 30–60 seconds
→ Auto Scaling event → new instance ready 3–4x faster
```

### AMI Types

```
AWS MARKETPLACE AMIs:
→ Pre-built by vendors (Bitnami, CentOS, etc.)
→ Some free, some paid (per-hour software license fee)
→ Examples: WordPress pre-configured, Jenkins ready, SAP HANA

AWS-PROVIDED AMIs:
→ Amazon Linux 2023 (most optimized for AWS, free, recommended)
→ Ubuntu 22.04/20.04 LTS
→ Windows Server 2022/2019
→ Red Hat Enterprise Linux (license fee)

COMMUNITY AMIs:
→ Shared publicly by other AWS users
→ FREE to use
→ ⚠️ SECURITY RISK: do not use from unknown sources
→ Could contain malware, backdoors
→ Only use from trusted, known publishers

YOUR CUSTOM AMIs:
→ Create from your running EC2 instance
→ Includes: OS + your software + your config
→ Stored in your account (private by default)
→ Can share with specific accounts or make public
→ Region-specific: must copy to other regions to use there
```

### Creating a Custom AMI (Golden Image Pattern)

```
PROCESS:
1. Launch base EC2 (e.g., Amazon Linux 2023)
2. Install everything: nginx, Node.js 18, PM2, your app
3. Configure: nginx config, environment, systemd services
4. Test: verify app starts correctly
5. Create AMI: EC2 Console → Actions → Image and Templates → Create image
   → This stops the instance briefly, takes snapshots of all EBS volumes
   → Creates AMI with those snapshots

RESULT:
→ AMI ID: ami-0abc123456789xyz
→ Use this AMI in your Launch Template
→ Auto Scaling Group uses Launch Template → all instances identical

GOLDEN IMAGE BEST PRACTICES:
→ Bake in security patches (update OS before creating AMI)
→ Don't bake in secrets (use Secrets Manager at runtime)
→ Version your AMIs: "myapp-v1.2.3-2024-01-15"
→ Automate AMI creation with CI/CD pipeline (EC2 Image Builder)
→ Deprecate old AMIs (but keep snapshots for rollback)
→ Share AMI to multiple regions via AWS RAM or copy

EC2 IMAGE BUILDER:
→ AWS service to automate AMI creation pipeline
→ Define: base image → software components → tests → distribute
→ Schedules automatic rebuilds (weekly OS patches)
→ Runs tests before publishing AMI
→ Distributes to multiple regions automatically
→ Free (pay only for EC2 instances used during build)
```

---

Launch Template

            Users

→ AWS service to automate AMI creation pipeline

sections.title: ⚖️ Load Balancing — ELB
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Why Load Balancing?
```
WITHOUT LOAD BALANCER:
Users → your-app.com → ONE EC2 instance (single point of failure)
→ Instance goes down → ALL users get error
→ Traffic spike → one instance overwhelmed → slow/crashes
→ Deploy new version → take instance down → downtime

WITH LOAD BALANCER:
Users → your-app.com → Load Balancer → multiple EC2 instances
→ Instance goes down → LB routes to healthy instances automatically
→ Traffic spike → LB distributes across all instances
→ Deploy → roll one instance at a time → zero downtime
→ Scale out → add instances → LB discovers them automatically
```

### The 3 Types of AWS Load Balancers

```
ALB — Application Load Balancer (Layer 7)
NLB — Network Load Balancer (Layer 4)
GLB — Gateway Load Balancer (Layer 3)
```

### ALB — Application Load Balancer (Most Common)

```
LAYER 7 = HTTP/HTTPS layer — understands the content of requests

WHAT ALB CAN DO (NLB cannot):
→ Route based on URL path:
   /api/*  → Target Group 1 (API servers)
   /admin/* → Target Group 2 (Admin servers)
   /*       → Target Group 3 (Frontend servers)

→ Route based on hostname:
   api.myapp.com   → API servers
   admin.myapp.com → Admin servers
   www.myapp.com   → Web servers

→ Route based on HTTP headers, query strings, method

→ Redirect: HTTP → HTTPS (no code change needed)
→ Return fixed response (403, 200 with custom body)
→ Authenticate users via Cognito or OIDC before routing

→ WebSocket support
→ HTTP/2 support
→ gRPC support

COMPONENTS:
Listener    → checks for connections on a port (80, 443)
Rule        → if condition → forward to target group
Target Group → set of targets (EC2, Lambda, IP, ECS tasks)
Health Check → pings targets, removes unhealthy ones

HEALTH CHECKS:
→ ALB pings each target on specified path (e.g., GET /health)
→ Expected response: 200 OK
→ If target fails N consecutive checks → marked unhealthy → removed from rotation
→ When healthy again → added back automatically
→ Your app MUST have a /health endpoint that returns 200

PRICING:
→ $0.008/hour per ALB (~$5.76/month)
→ + $0.008 per LCU-hour (Load Capacity Unit — based on connections/requests)
→ ~$15–50/month for moderate traffic

USE ALB FOR:
✅ Web applications (HTTP/HTTPS)
✅ Microservices with path-based routing
✅ Container-based apps (ECS, EKS)
✅ WebSocket applications
✅ When you need request-level features (headers, auth, redirect)
```

### NLB — Network Load Balancer

```
LAYER 4 = TCP/UDP layer — doesn't look inside packets, just routes them

CHARACTERISTICS:
→ Extreme performance: handles millions of requests per second
→ Ultra-low latency: microseconds (vs milliseconds for ALB)
→ Static IP address per AZ (ALB doesn't have static IP)
→ Preserves client source IP (ALB replaces with its own IP)
→ Supports: TCP, UDP, TLS, TCP_UDP
→ NO path-based routing (doesn't understand HTTP)
→ NO host-based routing

USE NLB FOR:
✅ Gaming (UDP, ultra-low latency)
✅ IoT (millions of concurrent connections)
✅ Real-time financial trading (microsecond latency critical)
✅ When you need static IP (for whitelisting on client firewalls)
✅ TCP/UDP applications (not HTTP)
✅ Extremely high throughput requirements

PRICING:
→ $0.006/hour per NLB (~$4.32/month)
→ + per NLCU-hour (similar to ALB)
```

### GLB — Gateway Load Balancer

```
LAYER 3 = IP packet level

Deploys, scales, and manages third-party virtual network appliances:
→ Firewalls (Palo Alto, Fortinet, Check Point)
→ Intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS)
→ Deep packet inspection

USE CASE:
All traffic → GLB → send to firewall appliance → inspect → GLB → destination
If you don't know what GLB is → you don't need it yet (enterprise use)
```

### ALB Target Groups

```
A Target Group = the set of servers ALB sends traffic to

TARGET TYPES:
Instances   → EC2 instances (register by instance ID)
IP addresses → Any IP (EC2, on-premise, containers with specific IPs)
Lambda      → Serverless functions (HTTP trigger via ALB)
ALB         → Another ALB (rare, for special architectures)

HEALTH CHECK SETTINGS:
Protocol:         HTTP or HTTPS
Path:             /health (your endpoint)
Healthy threshold: 3 (consecutive successes to mark healthy)
Unhealthy threshold: 3 (consecutive failures to mark unhealthy)
Timeout:          5 seconds (how long to wait for response)
Interval:         30 seconds (how often to check)

ALGORITHM:
Round Robin (default): requests distributed evenly
Least Outstanding Requests: send to instance with fewest active requests
                            (better for varied request durations)

STICKINESS (Session Affinity):
→ Route same user to same instance every time
→ Uses cookie (ALB-generated or application-generated)
→ Duration: 1 second to 7 days
→ Useful for: apps that store session in memory (not recommended — use Redis instead)
→ Breaks even distribution during scaling events
```

---

sections.title: 📈 Auto Scaling Group (ASG)
sections.rawMarkdown:
### What is an ASG?
```
ASG = a group of EC2 instances that scales automatically based on demand

YOU DEFINE:
→ Minimum instances (always running, floor)
→ Maximum instances (never exceed, ceiling)  
→ Desired capacity (target number right now)
→ Launch Template (which AMI, instance type, SG, IAM role)
→ Scaling policies (when to add/remove instances)

ASG DOES:
→ Maintains desired capacity (if instance dies → launches replacement)
→ Scales out (adds instances) when load increases
→ Scales in (removes instances) when load decreases
→ Registers new instances with ALB automatically
→ Spreads instances across AZs automatically (for HA)

ANALOGY:
ASG = a smart staffing agency for your servers
Tell it: "I need 2–10 servers. Add more when CPU > 70%. Remove when CPU < 30%"
Agency automatically hires/fires servers as needed. 24/7.
```

### Launch Template vs Launch Configuration

```
LAUNCH CONFIGURATION (OLD — deprecated):
→ Cannot modify after creation (must create new one)
→ Cannot use mixed instance types
→ Cannot use T unlimited
→ AWS is phasing this out

LAUNCH TEMPLATE (NEW — always use this):
→ Can have multiple versions
→ Supports mixed instances (Spot + On-Demand in same ASG)
→ Supports all instance features
→ Reusable across ASGs
→ Inheritance: create child template from parent (override only what changes)

LAUNCH TEMPLATE CONTAINS:
→ AMI ID
→ Instance type (or multiple types for mixed)
→ Key pair
→ Security groups
→ IAM instance profile
→ User data script
→ EBS volume configuration
→ Network configuration
→ Tags
```

### Scaling Policies — 3 Types

```
1. TARGET TRACKING SCALING (SIMPLEST — USE THIS FIRST)
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ "Keep average CPU at 50%"                              │
   │ ASG automatically adds/removes instances to reach 50%  │
   │                                                         │
   │ Pre-built metrics:                                      │
   │ → Average CPU utilization                              │
   │ → Average network in/out                               │
   │ → ALB request count per target                        │
   │                                                         │
   │ Custom metric example:                                  │
   │ → "Keep SQS queue depth at 100 messages per instance"  │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

2. STEP SCALING (MORE CONTROL)
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ Based on CloudWatch alarm breaches:                    │
   │                                                         │
   │ CPU > 70% → add 2 instances                           │
   │ CPU > 90% → add 5 instances (bigger emergency step)   │
   │ CPU < 30% → remove 1 instance                         │
   │ CPU < 20% → remove 2 instances                        │
   │                                                         │
   │ Good for: well-understood traffic patterns             │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3. SCHEDULED SCALING (PREDICTABLE PATTERNS)
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ "Every weekday at 8am → set desired to 10"            │
   │ "Every weekday at 8pm → set desired to 2"             │
   │ "Every Black Friday → set desired to 50"              │
   │                                                         │
   │ Good for: known traffic patterns, business hours apps  │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

PREDICTIVE SCALING (ML-BASED — NEW):
→ AWS analyzes last 2 weeks of load patterns
→ Predicts future load using ML
→ Proactively adds instances BEFORE load hits
→ Avoids the lag between load spike and scale-out
→ Combine with Target Tracking for best results
```

### ASG + ALB Integration

```
THE STANDARD PRODUCTION PATTERN:

Internet → ALB (public subnet, 2+ AZs)
           ↓
    Target Group (ASG registers instances here automatically)
    ↓             ↓             ↓
EC2 (AZ-a)   EC2 (AZ-b)   EC2 (AZ-c)
(private subnet) (private subnet) (private subnet)
           ↓
         RDS (Multi-AZ, private DB subnet)

HOW IT WORKS TOGETHER:
1. ASG launches new EC2 instance (Auto Scaling event or desired change)
2. EC2 runs User Data / boots from Golden AMI
3. ASG registers instance with ALB Target Group
4. ALB health check: GET /health → 200 OK
5. ALB starts routing traffic to new instance
6. Instance is now in full service

SCALE-IN PROTECTION:
→ Draining: before removing an instance, ALB stops sending NEW requests
→ Existing requests complete (deregistration delay: 300s default)
→ After drain period: instance terminated gracefully
→ Zero dropped requests during scale-in

WARM-UP PERIOD:
→ After instance launches → don't count it in scaling metrics yet
→ Give it N seconds to finish starting up
→ Prevents premature scale-out triggered by new instances still booting
→ Default 300s → set to actual startup time of your app
```

### ASG Lifecycle Hooks

```
Lifecycle hooks let you PAUSE the instance during launch or termination
to run custom actions.

LAUNCH HOOK (Pending:Wait state):
→ Instance starting → PAUSE before going live
→ Run: install extra software, pull config from S3, register with monitoring
→ Signal complete: aws autoscaling complete-lifecycle-action ...
→ Then: instance goes InService, gets traffic

TERMINATE HOOK (Terminating:Wait state):
→ Instance being terminated → PAUSE before actual termination
→ Run: drain connections, save state to S3, deregister from monitoring, publish metrics
→ Signal complete: aws autoscaling complete-lifecycle-action ...
→ Then: instance actually terminates

TIMEOUT: if you don't signal within timeout (default 1 hour) → continues anyway

USE CASES:
→ Custom health checks before accepting traffic
→ Warm up application caches during launch hook
→ Backup logs to S3 during terminate hook
→ Notify monitoring system of scale events
```

### ASG Key Settings and Gotchas

```
COOLDOWN PERIOD:
→ After scaling activity → wait N seconds before next scale activity
→ Prevents thrashing (scale out → scale in → scale out → ...)
→ Default: 300 seconds (5 minutes)
→ Target Tracking: manages this automatically (no cooldown needed)

TERMINATION POLICY (which instance to remove on scale-in):
Default: 
  1. Balance across AZs (remove from AZ with most instances)
  2. Remove oldest Launch Template (keep newest deployed version)
  3. Remove instance closest to billing hour (save money)

Custom options: OldestInstance, NewestInstance, ClosestToNextInstanceHour

INSTANCE REFRESH (rolling deployment):
→ Replace all instances in ASG with new Launch Template version
→ Rolling: replaces % at a time (e.g., 20% at a time)
→ Waits for health check before continuing
→ Pause if too many unhealthy → investigate + resume/cancel
→ USE THIS for deployments instead of manual instance replacement

MIXED INSTANCES POLICY:
→ ASG uses multiple instance types (not just one)
→ Primary: m5.large → fallback: m5a.large, m4.large, m5.xlarge
→ If primary Spot unavailable → use fallback → always have capacity
→ Base: X On-Demand + rest Spot (maintain baseline with reliability)
→ Huge cost savings for fault-tolerant workloads

GOTCHA: ASG does NOT replace unhealthy based on YOUR app health
→ By default: only replaces if EC2 fails EC2-level health check (ping fails)
→ If your app crashes but EC2 is up → ASG doesn't know → won't replace
→ FIX: enable ALB health check in ASG (if ALB marks unhealthy → ASG replaces)
→ OR: use ASG health check grace period + custom health check scripts
```

---

   │ CPU > 90% → add 5 instances (bigger emergency step)   │
   │ CPU < 30% → remove 1 instance                         │
   │ CPU < 20% → remove 2 instances                        │
   │                                                         │
   │ Good for: well-understood traffic patterns             │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3. SCHEDULED SCALING (PREDICTABLE PATTERNS)
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ "Every weekday at 8am → set desired to 10"            │
   │ "Every weekday at 8pm → set desired to 2"             │
   │ "Every Black Friday → set desired to 50"              │
   │                                                         │
   │ Good for: known traffic patterns, business hours apps  │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

PREDICTIVE SCALING (ML-BASED — NEW):
→ AWS analyzes last 2 weeks of load patterns
→ Predicts future load using ML
→ Proactively adds instances BEFORE load hits
→ Avoids the lag between load spike and scale-out
→ Combine with Target Tracking for best results
### ASG + ALB Integration

THE STANDARD PRODUCTION PATTERN:

Internet → ALB (public subnet, 2+ AZs)
           ↓
    Target Group (ASG registers instances here automatically)
    ↓             ↓             ↓
EC2 (AZ-a)   EC2 (AZ-b)   EC2 (AZ-c)
(private subnet) (private subnet) (private subnet)
           ↓
         RDS (Multi-AZ, private DB subnet)

HOW IT WORKS TOGETHER:
1. ASG launches new EC2 instance (Auto Scaling event or desired change)
2. EC2 runs User Data / boots from Golden AMI
3. ASG registers instance with ALB Target Group
4. ALB health check: GET /health → 200 OK
5. ALB starts routing traffic to new instance
6. Instance is now in full service

SCALE-IN PROTECTION:
→ Draining: before removing an instance, ALB stops sending NEW requests
→ Existing requests complete (deregistration delay: 300s default)
→ After drain period: instance terminated gracefully
→ Zero dropped requests during scale-in

WARM-UP PERIOD:
→ After instance launches → don't count it in scaling metrics yet
→ Give it N seconds to finish starting up
→ Prevents premature scale-out triggered by new instances still booting
→ Default 300s → set to actual startup time of your app
### ASG Lifecycle Hooks

Lifecycle hooks let you PAUSE the instance during launch or termination
to run custom actions.

LAUNCH HOOK (Pending:Wait state):
→ Instance starting → PAUSE before going live
→ Run: install extra software, pull config from S3, register with monitoring
→ Signal complete: aws autoscaling complete-lifecycle-action ...
→ Then: instance goes InService, gets traffic

TERMINATE HOOK (Terminating:Wait state):
→ Instance being terminated → PAUSE before actual termination
→ Run: drain connections, save state to S3, deregister from monitoring, publish metrics
→ Signal complete: aws autoscaling complete-lifecycle-action ...
→ Then: instance actually terminates

TIMEOUT: if you don't signal within timeout (default 1 hour) → continues anyway

USE CASES:
→ Custom health checks before accepting traffic
→ Warm up application caches during launch hook
→ Backup logs to S3 during terminate hook
→ Notify monitoring system of scale events
### ASG Key Settings and Gotchas

COOLDOWN PERIOD:
→ After scaling activity → wait N seconds before next scale activity
→ Prevents thrashing (scale out → scale in → scale out → ...)
→ Default: 300 seconds (5 minutes)
→ Target Tracking: manages this automatically (no cooldown needed)

TERMINATION POLICY (which instance to remove on scale-in):
Default: 
  1. Balance across AZs (remove from AZ with most instances)
  2. Remove oldest Launch Template (keep newest deployed version)
  3. Remove instance closest to billing hour (save money)

Custom options: OldestInstance, NewestInstance, ClosestToNextInstanceHour

INSTANCE REFRESH (rolling deployment):
→ Replace all instances in ASG with new Launch Template version
→ Rolling: replaces % at a time (e.g., 20% at a time)
→ Waits for health check before continuing
→ Pause if too many unhealthy → investigate + resume/cancel
→ USE THIS for deployments instead of manual instance replacement

MIXED INSTANCES POLICY:
→ ASG uses multiple instance types (not just one)
→ Primary: m5.large → fallback: m5a.large, m4.large, m5.xlarge
→ If primary Spot unavailable → use fallback → always have capacity
→ Base: X On-Demand + rest Spot (maintain baseline with reliability)
→ Huge cost savings for fault-tolerant workloads

GOTCHA: ASG does NOT replace unhealthy based on YOUR app health
→ By default: only replaces if EC2 fails EC2-level health check (ping fails)
→ If your app crashes but EC2 is up → ASG doesn't know → won't replace
→ FIX: enable ALB health check in ASG (if ALB marks unhealthy → ASG replaces)
→ OR: use ASG health check grace period + custom health check scripts

revisionPoints: → Minimum instances (always running, floor)
→ Maximum instances (never exceed, ceiling)
→ Desired capacity (target number right now)
→ Launch Template (which AMI, instance type, SG, IAM role)
→ Scaling policies (when to add/remove instances)
→ Maintains desired capacity (if instance dies → launches replacement)
→ Scales out (adds instances) when load increases
→ Scales in (removes instances) when load decreases

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — AMI + ASG + ALB
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: How does Auto Scaling work with a Load Balancer?**
```
"The ASG and ALB work together through Target Groups.

When ASG launches a new instance, it automatically registers that instance
with the ALB's target group. The ALB then runs health checks — typically
an HTTP GET to a /health endpoint. Once the instance passes the health check,
the ALB starts sending it traffic.

When ASG decides to terminate an instance (scale-in), it first tells the ALB
to deregister the instance. The ALB drains existing connections (default 300
seconds) without sending new requests. After draining, ASG terminates the
instance. Zero requests are dropped.

For the ASG to know when to scale, you set scaling policies based on metrics
— most commonly CPU utilization or ALB request count per target. Target
Tracking is the simplest: 'keep average CPU at 50%' and the ASG handles
the math of how many instances to add or remove."
```

**Q: Our app is having a Black Friday traffic spike. How do you prepare?**
```
"Multi-layered approach:

1. Before Black Friday (1 week ahead):
   → Load test with expected peak traffic (Artillery, Locust, k6)
   → Find the bottleneck: database? application? external API?
   → Fix the bottleneck, not just scale around it

2. Scheduled Scaling:
   → 'November 29 6am UTC → set desired to 50 instances'
   → Proactively have capacity ready BEFORE the spike hits
   → Don't wait for Auto Scaling to react — be ahead of it

3. Predictive Scaling (if already have historical data):
   → Enable predictive scaling on the ASG
   → Uses ML to proactively scale based on patterns

4. Database preparation:
   → Aurora Auto Scaling for read replicas (auto-adds replicas under load)
   → Or pre-add read replicas manually
   → Enable ElastiCache for frequently-read data
   → Test database connection pooling limits

5. CDN:
   → Ensure CloudFront caching is properly configured
   → Product images, JS/CSS — should be served from CloudFront, not EC2
   → Reduces load on origin by 70-90%

6. Pre-warming ALB:
   → If expecting massive spike: request pre-warming from AWS support
   → ALB scales automatically but has a warm-up period

7. Set max capacity with cost protection:
   → Max instances * instance_cost = maximum possible spend
   → Set hard max to prevent runaway scaling (and runaway bill)

8. War room: have team ready. Monitor CloudWatch dashboard in real-time."
```

**Q: What is the difference between ALB and NLB?**
```
"Layer 7 vs Layer 4 — that's the core difference.

ALB understands HTTP: it can route based on URL path (/api vs /admin),
hostname (api.myapp.com vs www.myapp.com), HTTP headers, query strings.
It can do redirects, return fixed responses, authenticate users via Cognito.
Latency is in the low milliseconds. This is the right choice for 95% of
web applications.

NLB operates at the TCP/UDP level — it doesn't look inside the packets,
just routes them based on IP and port. The payoffs: millions of requests
per second, microsecond latency, static IP addresses per AZ. Use NLB for
gaming (UDP), real-time financial applications, IoT with millions of
devices, or any case where you need the absolute minimum latency or a
static IP to whitelist on client firewalls.

For most web apps, APIs, and microservices: ALB. For extreme performance
requirements or non-HTTP protocols: NLB."
```

---

gaming (UDP), real-time financial applications, IoT with millions of
devices, or any case where you need the absolute minimum latency or a

For most web apps, APIs, and microservices: ALB. For extreme performance

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
AMI → EC2: every instance launches from an AMI
AMI → Auto Scaling Group: ASG uses Launch Template which references AMI
AMI → EC2 Image Builder: automates AMI creation pipeline

ALB → EC2/ECS/Lambda: forwards traffic to targets
ALB → Target Groups: traffic routing rules
ALB → ACM: SSL/TLS certificate attachment (HTTPS termination)
ALB → WAF: attach WAF to inspect/filter HTTP traffic
ALB → Cognito: user authentication before reaching app

ASG → EC2: manages fleet of instances
ASG → ALB: registers/deregisters instances from target groups
ASG → CloudWatch: reads metrics to decide when to scale
ASG → SNS: notifications on scaling events
ASG → Spot Fleet: mixed On-Demand + Spot instances

NEXT: S3 — Object Storage Deep Dive
The most used AWS service. More features than any other service.
```

---
---

blocks.content:
AMI → EC2: every instance launches from an AMI
AMI → Auto Scaling Group: ASG uses Launch Template which references AMI
AMI → EC2 Image Builder: automates AMI creation pipeline

ALB → EC2/ECS/Lambda: forwards traffic to targets
ALB → Target Groups: traffic routing rules
ALB → ACM: SSL/TLS certificate attachment (HTTPS termination)
ALB → WAF: attach WAF to inspect/filter HTTP traffic
ALB → Cognito: user authentication before reaching app

ASG → EC2: manages fleet of instances
ASG → ALB: registers/deregisters instances from target groups
ASG → CloudWatch: reads metrics to decide when to scale
ASG → SNS: notifications on scaling events
ASG → Spot Fleet: mixed On-Demand + Spot instances

NEXT: S3 — Object Storage Deep Dive
The most used AWS service. More features than any other service.
